branches.name: Sunni
branches.summary: The largest branch of Islam (~85-90% of Muslims). Follows the Sunnah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ as transmitted by his companions, recognises the first four caliphs (Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali) as rightly-guided, and accepts leadership through community consensus.

schools.name: Hanafi
schools.founder: Imam Abu Hanifa al-Nu'man (d. 150 AH / 767 CE), Kufa
schools.regions: Turkey, Balkans, Levant, Egypt (partly), Central Asia, Indian subcontinent, China
schools.description: Oldest of the four Sunni madhhabs. Known for extensive use of reasoning (ra'y), analogy (qiyas) and juristic preference (istihsan). Considered the most flexible on matters of contemporary jurisprudence.

schools.name: Maliki
schools.founder: Imam Malik ibn Anas (d. 179 AH / 795 CE), Madinah
schools.regions: North Africa, West Africa, Upper Egypt, Sudan, parts of the Gulf
schools.description: Distinguished by reliance on the practice of the people of Madinah ('amal ahl al-Madinah) as a primary source, alongside the Qur'an and Hadith. Compiled in the Muwatta.

schools.name: Shafi'i
schools.founder: Imam Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i (d. 204 AH / 820 CE), Egypt
schools.regions: Egypt, Levant, Yemen, East Africa, Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia), Kurdistan
schools.description: Systematised the science of usul al-fiqh (legal theory) in his Risala. Balances textual evidence with reasoning; widely followed in scholarly institutions.

schools.name: Hanbali
schools.founder: Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal (d. 241 AH / 855 CE), Baghdad
schools.regions: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, parts of UAE and Oman
schools.description: Smallest of the four schools, characterised by strict adherence to the explicit texts of the Qur'an and Hadith with limited use of independent reasoning.

branches.name: Shia
branches.summary: Roughly 10-15% of Muslims. Holds that leadership (Imamate) after the Prophet ﷺ belongs to Ali ibn Abi Talib and his descendants (Ahl al-Bayt). Subdivided primarily into Twelvers, Ismailis, and Zaydis.

schools.name: Twelvers (Ithna 'Ashariyya)
schools.founder: Doctrine formalised through the Imams, notably Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq (d. 148 AH / 765 CE)
schools.regions: Iran, Iraq, Azerbaijan, Bahrain, Lebanon, parts of the Gulf, Pakistan, India
schools.description: The largest Shia branch (~85% of Shia). Believes in twelve divinely-appointed Imams, the last of whom (Imam al-Mahdi) is in occultation. Follows the Ja'fari school of jurisprudence.

schools.name: Ismailis (Seveners)
schools.founder: Split from Twelvers after Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq; follows Isma'il ibn Ja'far
schools.regions: Pakistan, India, Central Asia, East Africa, Syria, global diaspora
schools.description: Recognise a living Imam (Aga Khan for Nizari Ismailis). Strong emphasis on esoteric (batini) interpretation of scripture alongside the outward (zahir).

schools.name: Zaydis (Fivers)
schools.description: Closest of the Shia branches to Sunni jurisprudence. Reject the doctrine of infallibility for Imams; recognise any qualified descendant of Ali and Fatima who rises against injustice as a legitimate Imam.

schools.name: Ja'fari fiqh
schools.description: Primary school of jurisprudence among Twelver Shia. Adds the rulings and teachings of the Ahl al-Bayt to the Qur'an and Sunnah as sources of law.
